title: Efficiency and monopolies
description: Practice what you've learned about natural monopolies in this exercise, including what makes a monopoly a natural monopoly, government regulation, and efficiency.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following describes why monopolists do not produce the allocatively efficient quantity, but a perfectly competitive firm does?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A monopolist charges a price higher than marginal cost, but a perfectly competitive firm’s price equals marginal cost.
A monopolist earns long-run profits, but a perfectly competitive firm does not.
A monopolist’s average total cost curve is horizontal, but a perfectly competitive firm’s average total cost curve is u-shaped.
A monopolist’s demand curve is horizontal, but a perfectly competitive firm’s demand curve is downward sloping.
A monopolist’s marginal cost curve is higher than its average total cost curve, but a perfectly competitive firm’s marginal cost is less than its average total cost curve.

assessment_items.question_content:
Selena’s Sandwiches operates in a monopoly and sells $100$ sandwiches for $\$10$ each. 

**Based on this information, which of the following can we be certain is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The marginal cost of the $100^{\text{th}}$ sandwich is less than $\$10$.
The socially optimal quantity in this market is $100$.
There is no deadweight loss in this market.

assessment_items.question_content:
Wuthering Flights is an airline that sells seats for domestic air travel in the nation of Hamsterville. This firm experiences decreasing the average cost for the entire range of its demand curve due to very high start-up costs associated with running an airline. 

**Which of the following must be true based on this information?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It can produce and supply flights at a lower cost per unit than if there were many smaller firms.
It is productively efficient for the government to break up this monopoly.
It is possible to eliminate deadweight loss by taxing this firm.
It produces a quantity that is both productively efficient and allocatively efficient.

assessment_items.question_content:
The market for electricity in Hamsterville is a natural monopoly. The government of Hamsterville wants to regulate Cricetidae Lighting and Power, the sole producer of electricity in Hamsterville. The firm’s cost curves and demand are shown here.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the profit-maximizing price and quantity that this firm will choose?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph depicts the relationship between cost, price, and quantity for a firm. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Cost, in dollars, and a horizontal axis labeled Quantity. Four curves and lines are displayed. The horizontal line labeled P 1 represents price. The downward-sloping line labeled D represents demand, and the downward-sloping line labeled M R represents marginal revenue. The curve labeled M C is horizontal at price P 1. The curve labeled A T C slopes downward and intersects the demand line, D, at quantities Q 1 and Q 2. Dashed horizontal lines extend from price levels P 1, P 2, P 3, and P 4 to the Y-axis. Dashed vertical lines extend from quantities Q 1, Q 2, and Q 3 to the X-axis. The intersections illustrate cost and revenue interactions, with price P 1 corresponding to quantity Q 2 at equilibrium.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains why the firm shown in this graph qualifies as a natural monopoly?**

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph depicts the relationship between cost, price, and quantity for a firm. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Cost, in dollars, and a horizontal axis labeled Quantity. Four curves and lines are displayed. The downward-sloping line labeled D represents demand, and the downward-sloping line labeled M R represents marginal revenue. The upward-sloping curve labeled M C starts low, increases as it moves to the right, and intersects the A T C curve and the demand line, D, at points along the quantity axis. The curve labeled A T C slopes downward and remains above the M C curve.

choices.content: The average total cost is decreasing at every quantity demanded.
The demand curve is greater than the marginal revenue curve.
There is no quantity that maximizes profit for this firm.
The marginal revenue curve crosses the quantity axis at a lower quantity than the demand curve.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best defines a natural monopoly?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a firm that has economies of scale over its entire range of demand
a firm that produces the quantity where marginal cost equals marginal revenue
a firm that is the sole owner of a key resource
a firm that only sells natural resources
a producer that minimizes average total cost instead of maximizing profit

assessment_items.question_content:
**If the government of Hamsterville grants the monopoly that controls the hamster food industry a lump sum subsidy, which curve is affected?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**If a government imposes a lump-sum tax on a monopoly, which curve is affected?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

The government of Maxistan has imposed a lump sum tax on the monopoly that controls the game controller industry.

**Which of the following is a likely outcome of the lump sum tax?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

The market for electricity in Hamsterville is a natural monopoly. The government of Hamsterville wants to regulate Cricetidae Lighting and Power, the sole producer of electricity in Hamsterville. The government wants to set a price that reduces deadweight loss but doesn’t want to have to subsidize the firm to keep it in business. The firm’s cost curves and demand are shown here.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What price should regulators set and what quantity will be sold?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph depicts the relationship between cost, price, and quantity for a firm. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Cost, in dollars, and a horizontal axis labeled Quantity. Four curves and lines are displayed. The horizontal line labeled P 1 represents price. The downward-sloping line labeled D represents demand, and the downward-sloping line labeled M R represents marginal revenue. The curve labeled M C is horizontal at price P 1. The curve labeled A T C slopes downward and intersects the demand line, D, at quantities Q 1 and Q 2. Dashed horizontal lines extend from price levels P 1, P 2, P 3, and P 4 to the Y-axis. Dashed vertical lines extend from quantities Q 1, Q 2, and Q 3 to the X-axis. The intersections illustrate cost and revenue interactions, with price P 1 corresponding to quantity Q 2 at equilibrium.

assessment_items.question_content:
The market for electricity in Frankland is a natural monopoly. The government of Frankland wants to regulate Gator Lighting and Power, the sole producer of electricity in Frankland. The government wants to set a price that eliminates deadweight loss and will provide the firm with a subsidy if necessary. The firm’s cost curves and demand are shown here.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/647ab9aeacb9d0f173f435495ae797cffd8328be)

**What price should the government set to eliminate deadweight loss?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: There is no price that eliminates deadweight loss.
